metadata.app: SPIKE Prime + FLL Adaptive Placement Assessment

placement.label: Level 1 Early Foundations
placement.rationale: Needs foundational support and scaffolded practice before advanced tasks.

teacherSummary: Alex Rivera (Grade 5) completed 24 adaptive questions with an overall weighted score of 62%. Recommended placement: Level 1 Early Foundations. Strongest domains: Programming Fundamentals and SPIKE Prime Block Coding. Developing domains: Robotics Math and Robot Behavior Prediction. General Robotics readiness is developing (51.3%). SPIKE Prime Block Coding readiness is ready (74.1%). Pybricks / Python readiness is ready (74.6%). FLL Competition readiness is emerging (42%). Build familiarity with hub ports, sensor modes, gyro readings, motor encoders, and firmware updates. Use keyed quick-swap mounts with alignment guides for faster and more reliable transitions.
familySummary: Alex Rivera is currently placed at Level 1 Early Foundations with a weighted score of 62%. They are showing strong progress in Programming Fundamentals and SPIKE Prime Block Coding, and the next growth focus is SPIKE Prime Hardware & Sensors and Attachments. This placement helps build confidence while preparing for higher-level SPIKE Prime and FLL challenges.
recommendedNextPlacement: Place in Pybricks / Python Foundations with guided practice in SPIKE Prime Hardware & Sensors and Attachments.

questionHistory.questionText: How far does a wheel travel in one complete rotation?
questionHistory.selectedText: It can't be calculated
questionHistory.correctText: The wheel's circumference (diameter × π ≈ diameter × 3.14)

questionHistory.questionText: Your robot drifts left even when you program it to go straight. What's a likely cause?
questionHistory.selectedText: All robots drift left sometimes
questionHistory.correctText: One wheel is different size or one motor is weaker

questionHistory.questionText: A FLL match is 2 minutes and 30 seconds. Why is time management critical?
questionHistory.selectedText: Time is just a guideline
questionHistory.correctText: Slow robots won't finish all missions, so you pick the highest-scoring ones you can complete

questionHistory.questionText: What is GitHub used for in robotics teams?
questionHistory.selectedText: To charge the battery
questionHistory.correctText: To store and share code, track changes, and work as a team

questionHistory.questionText: What does drive_base.straight(200) tell the robot to do?
questionHistory.selectedText: Drive forward 200 millimeters in a straight line
questionHistory.correctText: Drive forward 200 millimeters in a straight line

questionHistory.questionText: Someone suggests an idea you think won't work. What should you do?
questionHistory.selectedText: Listen, ask questions, explain your concerns kindly, and suggest alternatives together
questionHistory.correctText: Listen, ask questions, explain your concerns kindly, and suggest alternatives together

questionHistory.questionText: Your program has an If-then block that checks a motor encoder. The program never runs the inside of the If. What's a possible reason?
questionHistory.selectedText: The motor hasn't run yet so the encoder is still 0
questionHistory.correctText: The motor hasn't run yet so the encoder is still 0

questionHistory.questionText: You want your robot to beep 5 times. Which block helps you do this without copying the beep block 5 times?
questionHistory.selectedText: Repeat block
questionHistory.correctText: Repeat block

questionHistory.questionText: What's the difference between an active attachment and a passive attachment?
questionHistory.correctText: Active has a motor and moves, passive just sits there

questionHistory.questionText: Your gyro sensor reads 45 degrees. What does this mean?
questionHistory.selectedText: The robot has rotated 45 degrees from where it started
questionHistory.correctText: The robot has rotated 45 degrees from where it started

questionHistory.questionText: You want a robot to keep moving forward until a distance sensor detects something close. What blocks do you use?
questionHistory.selectedText: Repeat-until and sensor block
questionHistory.correctText: Repeat-until and sensor block

questionHistory.questionText: A worm gear is like a screw. Why is it good for pushing heavy things?
questionHistory.selectedText: It makes a motor much stronger while going slower
questionHistory.correctText: It makes a motor much stronger while going slower

questionHistory.questionText: You put a heavy sensor on the very front of your robot. What might happen?
questionHistory.selectedText: The robot goes faster
questionHistory.correctText: The front dips down and the robot might not move

questionHistory.questionText: A color sensor has dust on its lens. What's the safest way to clean it?
questionHistory.selectedText: Gently wipe it with a soft, dry cloth (or lens wipe)
questionHistory.correctText: Gently wipe it with a soft, dry cloth (or lens wipe)

questionHistory.questionText: If a motor rotates 360 degrees (one full spin), what happens to a wheel attached to it?
questionHistory.selectedText: The wheel's size determines the motion
questionHistory.correctText: The wheel completes one full rotation

questionHistory.questionText: Your robot has rubber tires on a slippery table. What happens compared to hard plastic wheels?
questionHistory.selectedText: Rubber has better grip and the robot is less likely to slip and spin out
questionHistory.correctText: Rubber has better grip and the robot is less likely to slip and spin out

questionHistory.questionText: In GitHub, a 'commit' is like a save point in a video game. What do you include?
questionHistory.selectedText: A message describing what changed and why
questionHistory.correctText: A message describing what changed and why

questionHistory.questionText: Bevel gears can move power at a 90-degree angle. What does that mean?
questionHistory.selectedText: The robot can only turn corners
questionHistory.correctText: One rotates up-down and the other rotates left-right

questionHistory.questionText: What should you avoid when handling motors?
questionHistory.selectedText: Dropping them or applying hard pressure to the shaft
questionHistory.correctText: Dropping them or applying hard pressure to the shaft

questionHistory.questionText: Your robot works fine in practice, but fails during the competition on the field. What's a common reason?
questionHistory.selectedText: Something is loose or not built strong
questionHistory.correctText: Something is loose or not built strong

questionHistory.questionText: You want to remember how many times your robot bumped into something. What should you use?
questionHistory.selectedText: A variable
questionHistory.correctText: A variable

questionHistory.questionText: Your robot has a color sensor. You want it to stop if it sees red. What kind of block do you need?
questionHistory.selectedText: If-then block
questionHistory.correctText: If-then block

questionHistory.questionText: A color sensor can work in two main modes. What are they?
questionHistory.correctText: Color mode and reflected light mode

questionHistory.questionText: Why might a team design a quick-swap system for attachments?
questionHistory.selectedText: So they can quickly change to different attachments for different tasks without tools
questionHistory.correctText: So they can quickly change to different attachments for different tasks without tools
